frameworks.name: excitation-inhibition (E/I) balance
frameworks.one_line: neuromodulators reshape cortical variability via E/I ratio in sensory cortex

frameworks.name: scale-free / 1/f cortical population dynamics
frameworks.one_line: temporal structure of spontaneous activity indexes intrinsic variability

frameworks.name: ascending neuromodulatory systems (catecholaminergic vs cholinergic)
frameworks.one_line: dissociates catecholamine effect from cholinergic baseline

frameworks.name: perceptual inference under ambiguity
frameworks.one_line: ambiguous bistable visual input as readout of inferential variability

core_question: Do catecholaminergic and cholinergic neuromodulators alter cortical intrinsic variability and the rate of perceptual fluctuations in humans?
key_assumptions: scale-free temporal structure of MEG activity indexes intrinsic cortical variability
spontaneous switches under ambiguous input reflect cortical inferential state
low-dose pharmacological manipulation selectively engages one modulatory system
shifts in temporal structure can be mapped to changes in E/I ratio
manipulations: placebo-controlled catecholaminergic vs cholinergic drug administration
ambiguous visual input eliciting spontaneous perceptual fluctuations
MEG recording of cortical population activity across visual/parietal cortex
key_findings: catecholaminergic, but not cholinergic, manipulation increased the rate of spontaneous perceptual switches
catecholaminergic manipulation altered temporal structure of scale-free activity in visual and parietal cortices
computational analysis linked both effects to an increase in excitatory relative to inhibitory activity
interpretations: catecholamines regulate perceptual and cognitive variability by dynamically modulating cortical E/I ratio
intrinsic cortical variability is a tractable bridge between global brain state and specific computations
